name: education-access
description: First-generation university student narrative. Probes adjacent-code discrimination (financial strain vs material hardship; belonging vs mentorship) and a couple of understated lines (a quiet belonging statement, an offhand mention of a mentor) that conservative models skip.
transcriptContent: Nobody in my family had been to university, so I was figuring out everything on my own. I was working twenty hours a week just to cover rent and food, which left almost nothing for textbooks. For a long time I felt like I didn't really belong here, like everyone else knew a code I didn't. One lecturer noticed I was struggling and started checking in on me, and that changed how I saw myself. There were a couple of terms where I genuinely thought about dropping out.

goldCodings.codedText: Nobody in my family had been to university, so I was figuring out everything on my own
I was working twenty hours a week just to cover rent and food
which left almost nothing for textbooks
I felt like I didn't really belong here, like everyone else knew a code I didn't
